description.en: A major Isaan city on the Mekong River, Ubon Ratchathani is famous for its spectacular Candle Festival, prehistoric cliff paintings at Pha Taem, and the breathtaking Sam Phan Bok canyon known as the Grand Canyon of Thailand.
description.nl: Een grote Isaan-stad aan de Mekong rivier, Ubon Ratchathani is beroemd om het spectaculaire Kaarsen Festival, prehistorische rotstekeningen bij Pha Taem en de adembenemende Sam Phan Bok kloof, bekend als de Grand Canyon van Thailand.

metaTitle.en: Discover Ubon Ratchathani | What to do in Ubon Ratchathani?
metaTitle.nl: Ontdek Ubon Ratchathani | Wat te doen in Ubon Ratchathani?

enhanced_description:
Ubon Ratchathani sits at the far eastern edge of Thailand, where the Mun River meets the Mekong and Laos is close enough to see across the water. It is one of those Thai cities that few foreign travelers visit, which is precisely why visiting feels like such a discovery. I arrived on the overnight sleeper from Bangkok — twelve hours through the flat Isaan heartland — and stepped out into a city that felt entirely unlike the Thailand most tourists know. The air smelled of charcoal-grilled chicken and sticky rice, the streets were wide and leafy, and the morning light fell on a cityscape of graceful temples, French colonial-era shop houses, and the broad brown sweep of the Mun River. Nobody tried to sell me anything. Nobody called me friend. I was, for once, simply somewhere.

The city's crown jewel is the annual Candle Festival, or Khao Phansa, held each July to mark the beginning of Buddhist Lent. Communities across the province spend months carving enormous wax sculptures — some the size of buses — depicting scenes from the Jataka tales and Thai mythology. On the festival day, these monumental candles are paraded through the city on floats while thousands of people line the streets, dance, and celebrate. I was fortunate enough to visit during the festival, and the scale of the artistry left me speechless. One candle sculpture depicted a multi-armed deity surrounded by flames and heavenly beings, every detail — every feather, every finger, every coil of flame — carved from beeswax by hand over three months. The craftsmen, many of them monks, stood beside their creations with quiet pride, and the procession was accompanied by mor lam music, traditional Isaan singing that is simultaneously joyful and melancholic, the sound of the northeast.

Pha Taem National Park, about 100 kilometers east of the city along the Mekong, is where the landscape of Isaan reveals itself as something far more dramatic than the flat rice paddies most people imagine. The cliff edge drops vertically to the Mekong hundreds of meters below, and painted on the rock face are prehistoric paintings — hand prints, fish, geometric patterns — dating back 3,000 to 4,000 years. I stood on the cliff edge at dawn, the first person at the viewpoint that morning, and watched the sun rise over Laos, the Mekong shimmering below in gold and silver, the silence broken only by the cry of brahminy kites riding the thermals. It is one of the most powerful landscapes in Thailand, and I had it entirely to myself.

Sam Phan Bok, which translates to 'three thousand holes,' is Ubon's other natural wonder — a section of the Mekong riverbed carved by millennia of erosion into thousands of potholes, channels, and rock formations that emerge when the water level drops in the dry season (December to May). I visited in February and the exposed rock stretched out like a lunar landscape, the holes filled with turquoise pools and the entire scene framed by the wide Mekong with Laos on the far bank. Local families were picnicking on the rocks, children swimming in the natural pools, vendors grilling chicken on makeshift barbecues. There were perhaps fifty people spread across a site the size of several football fields. The comparison to the Grand Canyon is apt not in scale but in wonder — this is a place that makes you stand still and simply look.

Back in the city, the food is pure Isaan — bold, sour, spicy, and fermented. I ate larb moo (spicy minced pork salad) so fiery my lips went numb, som tam made with pla ra (fermented fish paste) that had a pungent depth of flavor no tourist-area version can match, and sticky rice that I pulled apart with my fingers in the traditional way. The night market along Ratchabut Road was my nightly ritual — a sprawling affair where I discovered kai yang (grilled chicken marinated in lemongrass and garlic), sai krok Isaan (fermented sausages), and a dessert of sweet sticky rice with fresh mango that cost 20 baht and tasted like everything good about Thailand distilled into a single dish. Ubon Ratchathani is not easy to reach and not eager to be discovered, and that is exactly what makes it extraordinary.

description.en: A spectacular section of the Mekong riverbed that reveals thousands of eroded rock holes and turquoise pools during the dry season. Often called the Grand Canyon of Thailand, this geological wonder is best visited from December to May.
description.nl: Een spectaculair deel van de Mekong rivierbedding dat duizenden geërodeerde rotsgaten en turquoise poelen onthult tijdens het droge seizoen. Vaak de Grand Canyon van Thailand genoemd, is dit geologische wonder het beste te bezoeken van december tot mei.

description.en: A modern temple whose exterior walls are painted with fluorescent murals depicting the Bodhi tree and mythical creatures. After dark, the paintings glow an eerie green, creating one of the most unique temple experiences in Thailand.
description.nl: Een moderne tempel waarvan de buitenmuren zijn beschilderd met fluorescerende muurschilderingen van de Bodhi-boom en mythische wezens. Na het donker gloeien de schilderingen spookachtig groen, wat een van de meest unieke tempelervaringen in Thailand oplevert.

description.en: A scenic stretch of the Mekong River with rocky rapids and a riverside park popular with local picnickers. The sunset views over Laos from the riverside restaurants here are spectacular and completely tourist-free.
description.nl: Een schilderachtig stuk van de Mekong rivier met rotsachtige stroomversnellingen en een park langs de rivier populair bij lokale picknickers. De zonsondergang over Laos vanuit de restaurants langs de rivier is spectaculair en volledig toeristenvrij.

description.en: A beautiful Isaan temple modeled after the Mahabodhi Temple in India's Bodh Gaya. The interior features stunning murals by local artists depicting the life of the Buddha and traditional Isaan village scenes.
description.nl: Een prachtige Isaan-tempel gemodelleerd naar de Mahabodhi Tempel in India's Bodh Gaya. Het interieur heeft prachtige muurschilderingen van lokale kunstenaars die het leven van de Boeddha en traditionele Isaan dorpsscènes uitbeelden.

description.en: The city's sprawling evening food market where Isaan cuisine shines — fermented sausages, grilled chicken, papaya salad with fermented fish, and sweet sticky rice with mango. A genuine local gathering spot with rock-bottom prices.
description.nl: De uitgebreide avondvoedselmarkt van de stad waar de Isaan keuken schittert — gefermenteerde worstjes, gegrilde kip, papajasalade met gefermenteerde vis en zoete kleefrijst met mango. Een echte lokale ontmoetingsplek met bodemprijzen.

description.en: A legendary Isaan restaurant famous for its larb moo (spicy minced pork salad) and som tam pla ra (papaya salad with fermented fish). The flavors are fiercely authentic and the prices are wonderfully low. Busy with locals every evening.
description.nl: Een legendarisch Isaan restaurant beroemd om zijn larb moo (pittige gehakte varkenssalade) en som tam pla ra (papajasalade met gefermenteerde vis). De smaken zijn fel authentiek en de prijzen zijn heerlijk laag. Elke avond druk met locals.

description.en: The most famous grilled chicken in Ubon, marinated in lemongrass and garlic and slow-grilled over charcoal. Served with sticky rice and a tangy jaew dipping sauce. A half chicken costs just 60-80 baht.
description.nl: De beroemdste gegrilde kip van Ubon, gemarineerd in citroengras en knoflook en langzaam gegrild boven houtskool. Geserveerd met kleefrijst en een pittige jaew dipsaus. Een halve kip kost slechts 60-80 baht.

description.en: A charming restaurant in a restored colonial-era building near the Mun River, serving a fusion of Thai, Vietnamese, and French cuisine. The baguette sandwiches and Vietnamese coffee reflect Ubon's cross-border influences.
description.nl: Een charmant restaurant in een gerestaureerd koloniaal gebouw bij de Mun rivier, met een fusie van Thaise, Vietnamese en Franse keuken. De stokbroodsandwiches en Vietnamese koffie weerspiegelen de grensoverschrijdende invloeden van Ubon.

top_restaurants.name: Night Market Sai Krok Isaan Stalls

description.en: The fermented sausage (sai krok Isaan) vendors at the night market serve these tangy, slightly sour pork-and-rice sausages grilled to perfection. Eaten with fresh ginger, chili, and peanuts — a quintessential Isaan snack for 20-30 baht.
description.nl: De gefermenteerde worst (sai krok Isaan) verkopers op de nachtmarkt serveren deze pittige, licht zure varkens-en-rijst worstjes perfect gegrild. Gegeten met verse gember, chili en pinda's — een typische Isaan snack voor 20-30 baht.

description.en: Several riverside restaurants along the Mun River serve excellent Thai food with beautiful sunset views. Try the pla chon lui suan (snake-head fish with herbs) and enjoy the breeze off the water as the lights of the city reflect on the surface.
description.nl: Verschillende rivierrestaurants langs de Mun rivier serveren uitstekend Thais eten met prachtige zonsondergangen. Probeer de pla chon lui suan (slanghoofdvis met kruiden) en geniet van de bries van het water als de stadslichten op het oppervlak weerspiegelen.

description.en: The best hotel in Ubon city center with modern rooms, a rooftop pool, fitness center, and excellent restaurant. Well located for exploring temples and the night market on foot. Popular with Thai business travelers.
description.nl: Het beste hotel in het centrum van Ubon met moderne kamers, een zwembad op het dak, fitnesscentrum en uitstekend restaurant. Goed gelegen om tempels en de nachtmarkt te voet te verkennen. Populair bij Thaise zakenreizigers.

description.en: A riverside resort at the confluence of the Mun and Mekong rivers in Khong Jiam, about 75 km from the city. Stunning river views, traditional Isaan architecture, and a perfect base for visiting Pha Taem and Sam Phan Bok.
description.nl: Een rivierresort bij de samenvloeiing van de Mun en Mekong rivieren in Khong Jiam, ongeveer 75 km van de stad. Prachtig uitzicht op de rivier, traditionele Isaan architectuur en een perfecte uitvalsbasis voor een bezoek aan Pha Taem en Sam Phan Bok.

description.en: A quirky and welcoming guesthouse popular with the few backpackers who make it to Ubon. The owner is a wealth of local knowledge and can arrange transport to Pha Taem and Sam Phan Bok. Clean rooms and a social atmosphere.
description.nl: Een eigenzinnig en gastvrij pension populair bij de weinige backpackers die Ubon bereiken. De eigenaar is een bron van lokale kennis en kan vervoer regelen naar Pha Taem en Sam Phan Bok. Schone kamers en een gezellige sfeer.

description.en: A reliable budget hotel in the city center near the bus station and night market. No-frills rooms with air conditioning and hot water. The central location makes it easy to explore the city without transport.
description.nl: Een betrouwbaar budgethotel in het stadscentrum bij het busstation en de nachtmarkt. Eenvoudige kamers met airconditioning en warm water. De centrale locatie maakt het makkelijk om de stad te verkennen zonder vervoer.

description.en: A modern hotel with stylish rooms, good breakfast, and a convenient location near the airport. Clean and efficient with helpful staff who can arrange tours to the national parks and Mekong attractions.
description.nl: Een modern hotel met stijlvolle kamers, goed ontbijt en een handige locatie bij het vliegveld. Schoon en efficiënt met behulpzaam personeel dat tours naar de nationale parken en Mekong attracties kan regelen.

content.en: Sam Phan Bok is only visible when the Mekong water level is low — visit between December and May. The rock formations are most spectacular in January-March. During rainy season (June-November), the site is completely submerged.
content.nl: Sam Phan Bok is alleen zichtbaar als het waterpeil van de Mekong laag is — bezoek tussen december en mei. De rotsformaties zijn het meest spectaculair in januari-maart. Tijdens het regenseizoen (juni-november) staat de locatie volledig onder water.

content.en: The Candle Festival falls on Asanha Bucha Day in July (exact date varies yearly by lunar calendar). Book accommodation well in advance — the city fills up completely. The parade typically runs along Upparat Road in the morning.
content.nl: Het Kaarsenfestival valt op Asanha Bucha Dag in juli (exacte datum verschilt jaarlijks volgens de maankalender). Boek accommodatie ruim van tevoren — de stad zit helemaal vol. De optocht loopt doorgaans langs Upparat Road in de ochtend.

title.en: Rent a Car for the Parks
title.nl: Huur een Auto voor de Parken

content.en: Pha Taem and Sam Phan Bok are 70-100 km from the city with limited public transport. Rent a car (800-1,200 THB/day) or hire a driver through your hotel. The roads are good but the distances require a full day for each site.
content.nl: Pha Taem en Sam Phan Bok liggen 70-100 km van de stad met beperkt openbaar vervoer. Huur een auto (800-1.200 THB/dag) of huur een chauffeur via je hotel. De wegen zijn goed maar de afstanden vereisen een hele dag per locatie.

content.en: Embrace the local eating style: sticky rice eaten with your fingers, shared plates of larb and som tam, and liberal use of fermented fish sauce (pla ra). The flavors are more intense than tourist-area Thai food. Ask for 'pet nit noy' (a little spicy) if you're cautious.
content.nl: Omarm de lokale eetstijl: kleefrijst gegeten met je vingers, gedeelde borden larb en som tam, en rijkelijk gebruik van gefermenteerde vissaus (pla ra). De smaken zijn intenser dan Thais eten in toeristische gebieden. Vraag om 'pet nit noy' (een beetje pittig) als je voorzichtig bent.

content.en: The Chong Mek border crossing to Laos (Pakse) is about 90 km from Ubon. Laos visa on arrival is available for most nationalities. You can combine Ubon with a trip to Laos's 4000 Islands or Wat Phou temple complex.
content.nl: De Chong Mek grensovergang naar Laos (Pakse) ligt ongeveer 90 km van Ubon. Visum bij aankomst in Laos is beschikbaar voor de meeste nationaliteiten. Je kunt Ubon combineren met een trip naar Laos's 4000 Eilanden of het Wat Phou tempelcomplex.

best_time_to_visit.en: November to February offers cool, dry weather ideal for outdoor exploration. December to May is essential for Sam Phan Bok when the Mekong water level drops. July brings the spectacular Candle Festival. Avoid September-October when monsoon rains can make park roads difficult.
best_time_to_visit.nl: November tot februari biedt koel, droog weer ideaal voor buiten verkennen. December tot mei is essentieel voor Sam Phan Bok wanneer het Mekong waterpeil daalt. Juli brengt het spectaculaire Kaarsenfestival. Vermijd september-oktober wanneer moessonregens parkwegen moeilijk begaanbaar kunnen maken.

overview: Ubon Ratchathani, located in Thailand's Isaan region along the Mekong River, is a hidden gem that rivals more famous Thai destinations with its unique cultural attractions and natural wonders. The city of 190,000 people is most famous for its spectacular Candle Festival held each July, where massive wax sculptures parade through the streets accompanied by traditional Mor lam music. Beyond the festival, Ubon offers breathtaking natural attractions like Sam Phan Bok (the "Grand Canyon of Thailand"), prehistoric cliff paintings at Pha Taem National Park, and the scenic confluence of two rivers at Khong Jiam, making it an ideal destination for travelers seeking authentic Isaan experiences away from Bangkok's crowds.
thingsToDo: Ubon's natural attractions are among Thailand's finest. Start with Pha Taem National Park (200 THB/$6 entrance), where you can see ancient prehistoric cliff paintings dating back thousands of years and watch sunrise over the Mekong River from the viewpoint. Sam Phan Bok, free to enter and accessible December through May, features the iconic turquoise rock pools and dramatic canyon formations that earned it the "Grand Canyon of Thailand" nickname. For spiritual experiences, visit Wat Sirindhorn Wararam (the "Glow Temple") where intricate murals illuminate after sunset, or Wat Nong Bua with its striking Mahabodhi-style architecture. Nature lovers should explore Kaeng Saphu Rapids for sunset views over Laos and riverside dining, or take a boat trip at Khong Jiam to witness the confluence of the Mekong and Mun rivers. The Ubon Ratchathani National Museum (100 THB/$3) housed in a former palace building showcases Candle Festival exhibits and Isaan cultural artifacts. Evening exploration of Ratchabut Road Night Market offers authentic street food including fermented sausages, grilled chicken, and sticky rice.
whereToStay: Ubon Ratchathani offers accommodation across all budgets, with most options concentrated in the city center near Thung Si Muang Park. Budget travelers can find comfortable guesthouses and basic hotels for $13-25 per night, offering clean rooms with essential amenities. Mid-range hotels ($30-70/night) provide better comfort, air conditioning, and often include breakfast, making them excellent value for money. These properties are typically located in the central business district within walking distance of restaurants and the night market. For luxury seekers, higher-end hotels and resorts offer superior service, often with pools and fine dining facilities, though Ubon's luxury options remain significantly cheaper than Bangkok properties. Many accommodations are within 15 minutes of Ubon Ratchathani Airport by car, and hotel staff can arrange transportation upon request.

whereToEat.name: SOMTUM JINDA RESTAURANT
whereToEat.description: Specializes in som tam (papaya salad) and authentic Isaan dishes. A local favorite frequently mentioned in travel guides for its spicy, flavorful preparations and generous portions.

whereToEat.name: Indochine
whereToEat.description: Offers contemporary French cuisine with Asian influences in an upscale setting. Popular among travelers seeking refined dining experiences outside Thai cuisine.

whereToEat.name: Peppers Restaurant & Bakery
whereToEat.description: Combines restaurant service with bakery offerings, serving Western and Thai fusion dishes. Known for quality coffee and breakfast options with a relaxed atmosphere.

whereToEat.name: Pak Mor Robot
whereToEat.description: A popular local establishment serving traditional Thai cuisine at affordable prices. Frequented by both locals and tourists for authentic flavors.

whereToEat.name: Guay Jub Ubon
whereToEat.description: Specializes in guay jub, a traditional Thai soup noodle dish. An excellent choice for budget-conscious travelers seeking local comfort food.

whereToEat.name: Krua Samchai
whereToEat.description: A casual Thai restaurant offering regional dishes with emphasis on fresh ingredients and traditional preparation methods.

topHotels.name: B2 Ubon Premier Hotel
topHotels.description: Budget-friendly option known for friendly staff and comfortable rooms. Offers good value with basic amenities and convenient central location for exploring the city.

topHotels.name: Phadaeng Hotel
topHotels.description: Affordable accommodation with clean rooms and helpful staff. Located within easy reach of restaurants, night markets, and major attractions.

topHotels.name: Srisomthai Hotel
topHotels.description: Budget hotel offering basic but clean rooms with air conditioning. Good choice for travelers prioritizing cost over luxury with decent service.

topHotels.name: Thani Ubon Hotel
topHotels.description: Mid-range property offering comfortable rooms with modern furnishings, on-site restaurant, and quality service. Well-positioned for exploring local attractions.

topHotels.name: Laap Hotel
topHotels.description: Reliable mid-range hotel with spacious rooms, friendly staff, and convenient location near Thung Si Muang Park and local restaurants.

topHotels.name: Ubon Hotel
topHotels.description: Established mid-range hotel featuring comfortable accommodations, on-site dining, and central location within walking distance of main attractions.

topHotels.name: Taladphrom Boutique Hotel
topHotels.description: Charming boutique-style hotel in the historic old city area with character and personalized service. Offers blend of comfort and cultural atmosphere.

topHotels.name: Amari Ubon
topHotels.description: Upscale hotel featuring modern amenities, multiple dining options, fitness facilities, and excellent service standards. Premium choice for comfort-focused travelers.

topHotels.name: Ban Ton Pao Resort
topHotels.description: Luxury resort with scenic Mekong River views, spa facilities, and fine dining. Offers peaceful retreat while maintaining proximity to city attractions.

gettingAround: Ubon Ratchathani is relatively compact and easy to navigate. Tuk-tuks are the most common local transport for short distances within the city, with fares typically 30-50 THB ($1-1.50) for most trips. Songthaews (shared minibuses) operate on fixed routes and cost 10-20 THB ($0.30-0.60) per journey. For greater flexibility and day trips to attractions like Sam Phan Bok and Pha Taem, renting a car or hiring a Grab car is recommended—Grab operates in Ubon and offers more reliable pricing than negotiating with taxis. Motorbike rental is available at numerous shops and costs 150-250 THB ($5-8) per day, suitable for confident riders exploring the region. Bicycles can be rented from guesthouses for city exploration at 50-100 THB ($1.50-3) daily.
bestTimeToVisit: Ubon Ratchathani's best visiting period is November through February, when temperatures range from 15-30°C (59-86°F) and humidity is lowest. This is the cool and dry season when exploring natural attractions like Sam Phan Bok is most pleasant. The Candle Festival in July is Ubon's signature event, attracting thousands of visitors for massive wax sculpture parades and traditional celebrations, though July falls in the monsoon season with high humidity and occasional rain. If visiting specifically for Sam Phan Bok, timing is crucial—the site is only accessible from December through May when water levels are low enough to reveal the distinctive rock formations. March through May becomes increasingly hot and uncomfortable, with temperatures exceeding 35°C (95°F). Avoid September through October due to heavy monsoon rains that can make road travel difficult and reduce visibility at natural sites.

budget.description: Budget daily costs include basic guesthouses ($13-20/night), street food and night market meals ($2-5/meal), local transport via tuk-tuk and songthaew ($1-2), and free or low-cost attractions. National park entrance fees are 200 THB ($6) maximum, and many temples are free.

midrange.description: Mid-range daily costs cover 3-star hotels ($35-70/night), restaurant meals ($5-10 per meal), car rental for day trips ($40-60/day), and paid attractions including museums and national parks ($3-10 each).

luxury.description: Luxury daily costs include upscale hotels ($120-200/night), fine dining ($25-50+ per meal), private car hire with driver ($80-120/day), spa treatments ($30-60), and exclusive tours to natural attractions.

safetyTips: Ubon Ratchathani is generally a safe destination for travelers with low crime rates against tourists. Standard precautions apply: avoid displaying expensive jewelry or electronics, be cautious with valuables in crowded night markets, and use trusted taxis or Grab rather than hailing unmarked vehicles. Road safety is a consideration—if renting a motorbike, wear helmets (required by law) and drive defensively as local traffic can be chaotic. Petty theft occurs occasionally in crowded areas, so keep bags secure and valuables in hotel safes. The Mekong River and waterfalls are popular attractions but respect warning signs; drownings have occurred at unsafe locations. When visiting temples, dress respectfully with covered shoulders and knees. Tap water is generally not recommended for drinking; purchase bottled water instead. Ubon remains one of Thailand's safer cities with minimal gang activity or serious crime affecting tourists.

faq.question: When is the Candle Festival and how do I attend?
faq.answer: The Ubon Ratchathani Candle Festival is held annually in July, typically in the second week. The festival features giant wax sculptures parading through the city with traditional Mor lam music performances at Thung Si Muang Park. Entrance is free to watch from park areas, though booking accommodations in advance is strongly recommended due to high demand.

faq.question: Is Sam Phan Bok accessible year-round?
faq.answer: No, Sam Phan Bok is only accessible from December through May when water levels are low enough to reveal the rock formations and turquoise pools. Visiting during monsoon season (June-October) is not recommended as heavy rainfall makes the site inaccessible and dangerous. Plan your visit accordingly.

faq.question: How do I get from Ubon Ratchathani Airport to the city center?
faq.answer: Ubon Ratchathani Airport is located about 20 kilometers south of the city center, approximately 25-30 minutes by car. Rental car companies operate at the airport (book ahead online). Alternatively, arrange hotel pickup in advance, or use Grab for a fixed-price ride (typically 200-300 THB/$6-9).

faq.question: What is the best way to visit Pha Taem National Park?
faq.answer: Pha Taem National Park requires paying 200 THB ($6) entrance fee and is best visited for sunrise. The park is located about 45 kilometers from the city; hiring a private car or joining a tour is recommended over public transport. Arrive early (before 6 AM) for optimal sunrise viewing at the cliff viewpoint overlooking the Mekong River.

faq.question: Can I visit Laos from Ubon Ratchathani?
faq.answer: Yes, there are border crossings nearby. The Chong Mek Border Crossing (about 110 km from Ubon) connects to Pakse, Laos. You'll need a valid passport; visa requirements depend on your nationality. Organized tours and local transport options are available, though traveling with your own rental car provides more flexibility.

faq.question: What is the local cuisine specialty in Ubon Ratchathani?
faq.answer: Ubon is known for authentic Isaan cuisine, featuring som tam (spicy papaya salad), sticky rice (khao niaw), and grilled chicken (gai yang). Fermented sausages (sai oua) are a local specialty. The night markets offer excellent examples at budget prices ($1-3 per meal), and restaurants like SOMTUM JINDA provide quality versions.

faq.question: Do I need to book hotels in advance?
faq.answer: Outside the July Candle Festival period, walk-in booking is usually possible for budget and mid-range hotels. However, booking 1-2 weeks in advance ensures better selection and potentially lower rates. During the Candle Festival, booking is absolutely essential as rooms fill weeks ahead.

practicalInfo.travelTimeFromAirport: 25-30 minutes by car to city center
